title: AFSLs must fix reactive AR governance models: AFCA
full_text:
The Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) has called out licensees for shortcomings in regard to meeting their adviser supervision obligations.
AFSL obligations have been in the spotlight lately as the profession continues to deal with the fallout from the Shield and First Guardian fund collapses with ASIC sharpening its eye on compliance and conduct across the industry.
AFCA’s Systemic Issues Insights Report for the first half of the 2025-26 financial year identified instances of allegations of misleading representations, deficient advice processes and weaknesses in supervision frameworks on the part of licensees.
“These matters did not arise from an absence of regulatory obligation,” it said.
“Rather, they highlighted risks where monitoring frameworks rely primarily on reactive mechanisms, such as complaint escalation or external inquiry, rather than structured supervision of representative conduct.”
At the end of the day, licensees still have a legal obligation to put adequate governance in place to ensure appropriate conduct by their authorised representatives.
“While authorised representatives may engage directly with clients, responsibility for advice standards, conduct oversight and compliance architecture remains with the Australian Financial Services licensee.”
As for what they should be doing, AFCA laid out five ‘markers of excellence’ for licensee governance.
These include:
- Risk-based monitoring programs focused on representative activity and high-risk product cohorts,
- Clear documentation of supervision frameworks and escalation pathways.
- Structured review of advice file quality and client outcome consistency,
- Early identification of misconduct indicators through monitoring and complaint analysis, and
- Board or executive visibility over authorised representative supervision metrics and remediation actions.
“Where advice models involve complex investment structures, high-yield representations or concentrated distribution channels, supervision intensity should be proportionate to the risk profile.”
Moving away from a reactive approach to implement more proactive strategies was, above all else, the most crucial trend raised, particularly while the industry is facing increased scrutiny.
“This theme remains particularly relevant in the current market environment, where supervision of authorised representatives continues to attract significant regulatory and industry attention.”
author: Shy-Ann Arkinstall
heading: Regulation
page_title: AFSLs must fix reactive AR governance models: AFCA - Money Management
